risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Health clinics were first stratified by city zone (e.g. Center, North, East, West, South). Of the 57 health clinics that met inclusion criteria, 42 were randomly selected and randomized to treatment or control conditions... To select the 42 health centers, all centers were assigned random numbers in Excel and sorted from smallest to largest... Randomization was completed in STATA." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation process using random numbers in Excel and randomization in STATA, with stratification by city zone, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

_evidence.method_quote: Health clinics were first stratified by city zone (e.g. Center, North, East, West, South). Of the 57 health clinics that met inclusion criteria, 42 were randomly selected and randomized to treatment or control conditions, based on sample size calculations. To select the 42 health centers, all centers were assigned random numbers in Excel and sorted from smallest to largest; health centers were selected based on city zone and in order of their random number. Randomization was completed in STATA.
_evidence.method_summary: Clinics were stratified by city zone, assigned random numbers in Excel, sorted, and randomized in STATA.
context_clues: randomized to treatment or control conditions

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper describes sequence generation but does not state any mechanism for concealing allocation from recruiters. It says, "all centers were assigned random numbers in Excel and sorted from smallest to largest; health centers were selected based on city zone and in order of their random number" and that "Randomization was completed in STATA," but there is no explicit description of central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other concealment procedure. Comment: Because allocation concealment is not described, the recruiter’s ability to foresee the next assignment cannot be ruled out, so the risk is Unclear rather than Low or High.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation (“all centers were assigned random numbers in Excel and sorted from smallest to largest”; “Randomization was completed in STATA”) but does not state how allocation was concealed from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic." Comment: Personnel delivering/assessing the intervention were not blinded, and the study included multiple subjective outcomes (e.g. past-year IPV, reproductive coercion, safety planning, quality of life, nurses' attitudes/skills) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation. This constitutes positive evidence of lack of blinding with outcomes susceptible to performance bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Data were collected through a computer-assisted survey ... Participants completed the survey in a private space in the clinic ... Research assistants were available during this period in case the participant had any questions. Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic." Comment: The outcome assessment for the main participant outcomes was patient self-report, so the participant was the assessor. Because the study text does not indicate participants were blinded, and the outcomes include subjective/self-reported measures (past-year IPV, reproductive coercion, use of community-based resources, safety planning, quality of life), knowledge of allocation could influence measurement. The study also reports nurse-reported subjective outcomes, and research assistants were explicitly unblinded, so the most vulnerable outcomes are at high risk of detection bias.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Data were collected through a computer-assisted survey which has been shown to improve response rates to sensitive behaviors [41]. Participants completed the survey in a private space in the clinic and were able to listen to the questions in Spanish through headphones and answer on the keyboard. Research assistants were available during this period in case the participant had any questions. Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic.

outcomes.label: knowledge, attitudes, and skills among nurses

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article does not report CONSORT-style participant flow or per-arm analysed numbers for the participant outcomes of interest, so post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes cannot be quantified. It only states that analyses will use intention-to-treat first, then per protocol, and that missing data may be handled with multiple imputation. Comment: Because no actual randomized-versus-analysed counts, loss percentages, or reasons for outcome-specific missingness are provided, the risk of attrition bias for the outcomes of interest cannot be judged as low or high from the supplied text. The only numeric dropout statement refers to nurses/staff ('26% staff dropped out primarily due to staff turnover at clinics'), which is process-evaluation/provider attrition rather than the participant outcome data.

_evidence.notes: No CONSORT-style flow or per-arm analysed numbers were reported in the provided text. The article states analyses will first use an intent-to-treat approach, followed by a per protocol analysis. The only attrition-related numeric detail given is that nurse staff dropout was '26% staff dropped out primarily due to staff turnover at clinics,' which refers to nurses/process evaluation rather than the primary participant outcome data.
